answers.answer: B) C -> A -> B. Reason: Constraints are: (1) A before B; (2) C before A; (3) B and C cannot be simultaneous; (4) one at a time. From (2): C must come before A. From (1): A must come before B. Therefore: C -> A -> B. Option A violates (2). Option C violates (1). Option D violates both (1) and (2).

CAUSAL ANALYSIS:

**Claim 1: Code reviews -> slower shipping**
(a) Causation NOT established
(b) Confounding variables: Team size changes, feature complexity, holiday season, other process changes, staffing changes
(c) Reverse causation: Maybe teams slowed down for other reasons, and code reviews were added IN RESPONSE to incidents
(d) Needed data: A/B test (some teams keep old process), control for feature complexity, track review time vs. total cycle time, compare to industry benchmarks

**Claim 2: Unit tests -> fewer incidents**
(a) Causation NOT established
(c) Reverse causation: Teams with fewer incidents may have MORE time to write tests (success breeds best practices)
(b) Confounding variables: Team skill/experience, codebase stability, product maturity, testing culture, monitoring quality
(d) Needed data: Randomized controlled trial, track teams BEFORE adopting tests, control for team seniority and codebase age

**Claim 3: Kubernetes -> higher costs**
(a) Causation NOT established
(b) Confounding variables: Traffic growth, new features, data growth, team expansion, cloud provider price increases, inefficient K8s resource allocation
(c) Reverse causation: Maybe costs were already rising, K8s adoption was unrelated
(d) Needed data: Cost per request/GB before/after (normalized), compare to traffic growth, analyze K8s resource utilization vs. provisioned

**Claim 4: Copilot -> more PRs**
(a) Causation NOT established
(b) Confounding variables: Developer skill (better devs use Copilot more), project phase (maintenance vs. greenfield), team assignment bias
(c) Reverse causation: More productive developers may be EARLY ADOPTERS of Copilot (selection bias)
(d) Needed data: Before/after Copilot adoption per developer, randomized rollout, control for project type and developer seniority, measure CODE QUALITY not just quantity

**GENERAL PATTERN:**
All four claims commit the "correlation != causation" fallacy. To establish causation, need:
1. Randomized controlled trials (A/B tests)
2. Before/after measurements with controls
3. Account for confounding variables
4. Rule out reverse causation
5. Measure the RIGHT metric (e.g., PR quality, not just quantity)
